original_hypothesis.title: AVM-Delegated veNFT Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked in AutoVotingEscrow Contracts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bribe.getReward() sends rewards to ownerOf(tokenId) which returns the AutoVotingEscrow (AVE) instance address for AVM-managed tokens. The check `_owner == avm` compares the AVE address against the AutoVotingEscrowManager address — always false. Rewards are transferred to the AVE contract which has zero ERC20 recovery functions. Additionally, setOriginalOwner in the manager is a no-op (empty body), and VotingEscrow._transferFrom's `_to == avm` check never triggers because the actual transfer destination is the AVE instance, not the manager. All bribe rewards for AVM-managed tokens are permanently lost.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a double-layered integration failure affecting every user who enables auto-voting. The AVE address != AVM manager address comparison guarantees the wrong code path. Even if patched, the no-op setOriginalOwner provides no fallback.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOf returns AVE address; `_owner == avm` compares AVE vs Manager (always false); safeTransfer sends to AVE with no recovery

preconditions: User has enabled auto-voting for at least one veNFT
The AVM-managed veNFT has voted for pools with bribe rewards
gaugeManager calls getReward for the tokenId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: AutoVotingEscrow holds NFTs but has no ERC20 withdrawal. Bribe.getReward sends tokens to ownerOf which is the AVE.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: setOriginalOwner is empty (line ~248 in AutoVotingEscrowManager); no admin action can fix already-locked funds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — every bribe claim for AVM tokens triggers this loss

operational_questions: How many tokens are currently managed by AVM instances?
Have any bribe getReward calls already been made for AVM-managed tokens?
validation_requirements: Verify ownerOf(tokenId) returns AVE address, not AVM manager address
Confirm AutoVotingEscrow has no ERC20 transfer function
Verify setOriginalOwner function body is empty

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Any user calls enableAutoVoting(tokenId) — normal protocol usage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: AVM executor votes the token via performLockAction (normal operation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: gaugeManager calls getReward — rewards sent to AVE contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens are permanently locked in AVE with no recovery path
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this occurs during normal protocol operation

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All bribe rewards for AVM-delegated tokens are permanently lost
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: AVM feature becomes a value trap — users lose all bribe income
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without contract upgrade; locked funds in immutable AVE contracts
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All bribe reward distribution for AVM-managed veNFTs

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None required — this is a systemic bug, not an exploit

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC20 balances accumulating in AutoVotingEscrow contracts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users enabling auto-voting stop receiving bribe rewards

validation_result.mechanism: Incorrect AVM address comparison routing rewards to unrecoverable contract
validation_result.impact_summary: All bribe rewards earned by AVM-delegated veNFTs are permanently locked in AutoVotingEscrow contracts, resulting in a total loss of bribe yield for auto-voting users.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The vulnerability stems from a critical integration mismatch between the Bribe contract and the AutoVotingEscrowManager architecture. When a user enables auto-voting via `AutoVotingEscrowManager.enableAutoVoting()`, their veNFT is transferred to an individual `AutoVotingEscrow` (AVE) contract instance, not the `AutoVotingEscrowManager` itself.

When `Bribe.getReward()` is called to distribute rewards, it retrieves the current owner of the veNFT via `IVotingEscrow(ve).ownerOf(tokenId)`. It then checks if `_owner == avm` (where `avm` is the `AutoVotingEscrowManager` address). Because the veNFT is held by the AVE instance and not the manager, `_owner == avm` evaluates to `false`. Consequently, the Bribe contract assumes the AVE instance is a regular user and transfers the ERC20 bribe rewards directly to the AVE contract's address.

The `AutoVotingEscrow` contract does not implement any functions to withdraw, transfer, or recover ERC20 tokens. Therefore, any bribe rewards sent to it are permanently locked.

Furthermore, even if the `_owner == avm` check were somehow bypassed or corrected, the fallback mechanism is also broken: `AutoVotingEscrowManager.setOriginalOwner()` is an empty function (`{}`), meaning the `originalOwner` mapping is never populated and always returns `address(0)`. Additionally, `VotingEscrow._transferFrom()` only attempts to call `setOriginalOwner` if `_to == avm`, which is false during AVM delegation since the destination is the AVE instance.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The provided code perfectly matches the hypothesis. `Bribes.sol` contains the flawed `_owner == avm` check. `AutoVotingEscrowManager.sol` contains the empty `setOriginalOwner` function and the logic that transfers veNFTs to `AutoVotingEscrow` instances. `AutoVotingEscrow.sol` lacks any ERC20 recovery functions.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The check `_owner == avm` fails because `_owner` is the AutoVotingEscrow instance, while `avm` is the AutoVotingEscrowManager. The rewards are then transferred to `_owner` (the AVE instance).

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
votingEscrow.transferFrom(msg.sender, address(target), tokenId);
        target.acceptLock(msg.sender, tokenId); // insert to token Ids here
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The veNFT is transferred to `target` (an AutoVotingEscrow instance), making it the owner of the token.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: function setOriginalOwner(uint256 _tokenId, address _user) external onlyVotingEscrow {} // implement this
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function is empty, meaning the `originalOwner` mapping is never updated, breaking any logic that relies on it.
This block is never executed because `_to` is the AutoVotingEscrow instance, not the `avm` (manager).

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: 1. User calls `AutoVotingEscrowManager.enableAutoVoting(tokenId)`. 2. The manager transfers the veNFT to an `AutoVotingEscrow` instance. 3. Later, `gaugeManager` calls `Bribe.getReward(tokenId)`. 4. `Bribe` fetches `ownerOf(tokenId)`, which returns the `AutoVotingEscrow` instance address. 5. `Bribe` checks if the owner is the `avm` (manager). It is not. 6. `Bribe` transfers the ERC20 reward to the `AutoVotingEscrow` instance. 7. The tokens are permanently locked as `AutoVotingEscrow` has no ERC20 transfer functions.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is highly realistic and occurs automatically during normal protocol operation. Any user who utilizes the AVM feature will unknowingly have their bribe rewards sent to an unrecoverable contract. No malicious actor is required; the system naturally traps user funds.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. All bribe rewards for all users utilizing the AutoVotingEscrowManager are permanently lost. This completely breaks the economic incentive for using the AVM and results in direct, unrecoverable financial loss for users.
recommended_mitigations: In `Bribes.sol`, update `getReward` to correctly identify AVM-managed tokens. Instead of checking `_owner == avm`, check if the token is managed by the AVM using `IAutoVotingEscrowManager(avm).tokenIdToAVMId(tokenId) > 0`.
If the token is AVM-managed, retrieve the original owner using `IAutoVotingEscrowManager(avm).getOriginalOwner(tokenId)` instead of the unpopulated `originalOwner` mapping.
Implement `setOriginalOwner` in `AutoVotingEscrowManager.sol` or remove it entirely if `getOriginalOwner` (which reads from the AVE instance) is sufficient.
Add an emergency ERC20 recovery function to `AutoVotingEscrow.sol` restricted to the manager or protocol owner to rescue any already-locked bribe rewards.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow, Voter, Bribe, and AutoVotingEscrowManager. 2. Create a veNFT lock for User A. 3. User A calls `enableAutoVoting`. 4. Simulate an epoch where the AVM votes and earns bribes. 5. Call `Bribe.getReward` for User A's tokenId. 6. Assert that User A receives 0 bribes and the AutoVotingEscrow instance contract balance increases by the reward amount.
